coredata.dc:description: Producing hydrogen from solar energy and seawater via photoelectrochemical (PEC) technology is an attractive approach for sustainable energy conversion. However, there are still huge challenges due to the unfavorable chlorine evolution reaction (CER). Herein, a strategy was proposed to inhibit the CER via constructing an S, N co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CDs) splicing layer (SNCL) on a TiO2 nanoarray (NA) photoanode. The produced SNCL 5/TiO2 NA photoanode obtained a CER faradaic efficiency (FE) of 35.6 %, far below that of the original TiO2 NA sample (88.3 %). Moreover, it can maintain its photocurrent density, exhibiting a corrosion resistance under seawater conditions. Further enhancing the sulfur content within SNCL, the FE (CER) could be further reduced to merely 19.5 %. Meticulous characterization and simulation reveal that the SNCL can isolate Cl− ions and the TiO2 NA catalyst, giving rise to inhibition of the harmful CER. This work not only provides a potential anode material for PEC seawater splitting, but also offers a directive for developing PEC catalysts with CER-inhibiting performance in subsequent research.

head.ce:title: Chlorine repulsion layer via splicing of S, N co-doped carbon dots for photoelectrochemical seawater splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Ship-Machinery Maintenance and Manufacture for Ministry of Transport, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, 116026, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Ship-Machinery Maintenance and Manufacture for Ministry of Transport

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Ship-Machinery Maintenance and Manufacture for Ministry of Transport, Dalian Maritime University, Dalian, 116026, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Producing hydrogen from solar energy and seawater via photoelectrochemical (PEC) technology is an attractive approach for sustainable energy conversion. However, there are still huge challenges due to the unfavorable chlorine evolution reaction (CER). Herein, a strategy was proposed to inhibit the CER via constructing an S, N co-doped carbon dots (S, N-CDs) splicing layer (SNCL) on a TiO2 nanoarray (NA) photoanode. The produced SNCL 5/TiO2 NA photoanode obtained a CER faradaic efficiency (FE) of 35.6 %, far below that of the original TiO2 NA sample (88.3 %). Moreover, it can maintain its photocurrent density, exhibiting a corrosion resistance under seawater conditions. Further enhancing the sulfur content within SNCL, the FE (CER) could be further reduced to merely 19.5 %. Meticulous characterization and simulation reveal that the SNCL can isolate Cl− ions and the TiO2 NA catalyst, giving rise to inhibition of the harmful CER. This work not only provides a potential anode material for PEC seawater splitting, but also offers a directive for developing PEC catalysts with CER-inhibiting performance in subsequent research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Full exploitation of solar energy can offer a useful means to solve the energy crisis [1–3]. However, it must be converted into secondary energy for subsequent industrial applications because of its diffuse and intermittent nature, which hinders its storage and transport [4]. The photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting technology, which could convert solar energy into hydrogen fuel, can satisfy this requirement [5]. Nevertheless, current reported PEC energy systems mainly use ultrahigh-purity water as feedstock, leading to excessive reliance on scarce fresh water supplies, which constrains their large-scale industrial application [6,7]. Therefore, developing abundant seawater resources (approximately 97 % of the water on earth) as the infinite feedstock in PEC process is a strong need for the future hydrogen production industry. Unlike freshwater, natural seawater contains a range of inorganic ions, which may create a complex reaction environment in PEC seawater splitting process. Cl− is the predominant anion component among them, with a high concentration of 0.5 M [8]. It could trigger an undesirable chlorine evolution reaction (CER), leading to an accumulation of the active chlorine species. They typically exhibit corrosiveness and biological toxicity, posing substantial challenges to marine environment and ecology [9]. Moreover, the invasion of them could cause a corrosion and inactivation of PEC equipment, which renders the PEC seawater splitting technology further deviating from its economic goals [10]. Unfortunately, the CER is favourable for competing with the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Because it involves a simple two-electron redox process with a kinetic advantage for the four-electron OER, requiring a lower overpotential [11]. Therefore, studies on inhibiting the undesirable CER are crucial for large-scale industrialization of PEC seawater splitting.

In electrocatalytic seawater splitting field, it is a feasible method to inhibit the CER by simply adjusting the electrolyte composition, based on the “alkaline design criterion” that no interfering CER occurs when the anode has an overpotential less than 480 mV under alkaline conditions [12,13]. Nevertheless, this criterion does not apply to the PEC seawater splitting process, since the valence band (VB) potential of most anodic photocatalysts is well above this value. Designing and preparing specialized anodic materials for seawater-splitting is another viable method [4,14]. In current studies, the strategies of the anode optimization for inhibit the CER include developing novel OER-selective catalysts [15], introducing a Cl− repulsion layer and so on [16]. For the PEC seawater splitting process, constructing a Cl− repulsion layer is the most appropriate and attractive solution among them. Because it can provide a barrier between Cl− ions and the anodic photocatalyst, aiming to achieve a process similar to freshwater splitting on the anode surface [17]. Therefore, this strategy can be developed on the basis of the well-known photocatalysts without worrying about their water splitting ability. Currently common Cl− repulsion layers include anion layers and metal oxide layers. However, anion layers, such as CO3 2−and SO4 2−, pose problems regarding their compatibility with photocatalysts, which may potentially affect the light-absorption capacity of the PEC catalysts. For metal layers, there are severely limited by their high cost and scarce precursors in large-scale industrial applications [16]. Thus, it is necessary to develop an inexpensive, widely sourced and simple-to-produce substitute for constructing the Cl− repulsion layer.

Carbon dots (CDs) can support various negatively charged functional groups, which enable them to possess the potential for repelling chloride ions [18]. Meanwhile, there are many advantages of them such as chemical inertness [19], environmental friendliness [20], simple synthesis, adjustable structures and good electrical properties [21]. In past reports, CDs functionalized with negatively charged groups have been demonstrated to effectively repel anions [22], applying in environmental treatment and product separation [18,23]. For PEC energy conversion field, numerous studies have indicated that CDs combine well with many well-known photocatalysts and can play different roles. In addition, the stability of the chlorine-repelling layer is of utmost importance, which can significantly influence the overall efficiency and durability of the PEC system. The functional groups on the surface of CDs can cross-link and stitch together to form a stable structure [21]. This unique property of CDs is highly beneficial for their application in PEC systems. Therefore, CDs may be an ideal material for forming a chlorine-repulsive layer. However, up to now, the potential of CDs to inhibit the CER in PEC seawater splitting field has rarely been explored. It is still a lack of follow-up researches and in-depth exploration of the mechanism [24,25]. Consequently, at present, the basic research on using CDs to construct a chlorine-repelling layer on photoanode for seawater splitting and discussing the mechanism of its CER-inhibiting capability has dual values in practical application and theory.

In this work, S, N co-doped CDs (S, N-CDs) were prepared as the precursors to build a S, N-CDs splicing layer (SNCL) on a TiO2 nanorod array (NA) photoanode. A high-temperature splicing process was carried out to enhance the bonding between the SNCL and TiO2 NA, effectively improving the performance stability and corrosion resistance of the SNCL/TiO2 NA photoanode. The constructed SNCL on anode exhibited a Cl− repelling ability, hence dramatically inhibiting the CER activity during PEC seawater splitting process. Moreover, the CER inhibiting mechanism of SNCL was investigated from the perspective of the regulatory effect on the EDL structure of the photoanode. This work not only provides a practicable method for producing eco-friendly and corrosion-resistant photoanodes for seawater splitting but also provides insights and a theoretical foundation for subsequent design of Cl− repulsion layers on photoanode.

Titanium butoxide (TBOT), thionine and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) were obtained from Macklin Chemistry Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride (NaCl), thiourea, and citric acid were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Reagent Co. Ltd. (TianJin, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was obtained from Shenyang Shenshi Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shenyang, China). All the materials employed in this study were used without further purification. Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass was purchased from Huanan Xiangcheng Co. Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). The deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experimental process.

S, N-CDs were synthesized via a microwave method. Specifically, citric acid (0.1 mol) and thiourea (0.1 mol) were dissolved in 10 mL of DI water to form a transparent solution. The mixed solution was then heated for 10 min in a 600 W microwave. After cooling to room temperature, brown solid products formed and dissolved in DI water again to obtain a crude S, N-CDs solution. The impurities were removed by filtration (200 nm filter) and DI water dialysis (100–500 Da cutoff dialysis membrane). The purified S, N-CDs powder was finally obtained by lyophilization.

Sx, N-CDs were also synthesized via the same procedure, where x is the molar ratio of thiourea to citric acid (x:1). S1, N-CDs and S, N-CDs are the same samples. N-CDs were synthesized via the same procedure, except for the use of citric acid (0.1 mol) and urea (0.1 mol) as ingredients.

TiO2 NA photoanodes were prepared via a hydrothermal process. In a typical synthesis, 150 μL of TBOT, 2 mL of HCl (10 M) and 2 mL of DI water were mixed in an ice-water bath and transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Next, a FTO conducting glass substrate (1 × 3 cm2) was immersed in the mixed solution, in which the substrate was leaning against the autoclave wall and tilted at an angle. Afterwards, the sealed autoclave was heated to 160 °C for 10 h. Finally, the prepared TiO2 NA sample was washed with DI water and annealed at 450 °C for 3 h.

10 mL of S, N-CDs solution at a concentration of y g L−1 (0.25, 0.5, 1.5, 2.5 and 5 g L−1) was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The prepared TiO2 NA photoanode samples were immersed in the solution with the conductive side facing up. Then the autoclaves were heated to 160 °C for 10 h. After cooling in air, the obtained samples were heated to 500 °C with a rate of 5 °C min−1 and preserved for 6 h in a N2 atmosphere. The final products were named SNCL y/TiO2 NA.
Photoanodes containing the other Sx, N-CDs with a concentration of 5 g L−1 were produced via the same method and named SxNCL/TiO2 NA.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were performed on a Zeiss Supra 55 Sapphire microscope and a JEOL JEM-2100F, respectively. The phase structures of the samples were analyzed via X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Rigaku RINT-2000 with Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 40 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was carried out on a PerkinElmer Frontier I spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB250Xi. UV–visible absorption spectra and UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were collected on a VARIAN UV1006M031. Thermogravimetric (TG) and derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) analyses were conducted on a METTLER TOLEDO TGA/DSC 1/1600. The zeta potential of the samples was determined on an Anton Paar surpass3. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on an Edinburgh FS5.

All the PEC tests were performed in a three-electrode configuration, using a platinum foil as the counter electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl/KCl electrode as the reference electrode and the prepared photoanodes as the working electrode. Simulated seawater (SSW, 0.5 M NaCl) and 0.5 M NaSO4 solution were used as the electrolyte. The light source was a 300 W Xe lamp with an AM 1.5G filter and the optical power density was 100 mW cm−2. Additionally, for the convenience of measuring the CER products and the stability of SNCL under high current density, a 300 W xenon lamp with an ultraviolet filter was also utilized as the light source and the optical power density was 280 mW cm−2. Its energy spectrum is provided in Fig. S1. All the potentials measured in this work were converted to RHE based on the Nernst equation below:
(1)
E
R H E
=
E
A g / A g C l
+ 0.0591 × p H + 0.1976

The evaluation test for H2 production activity under practical application conditions was conducted in a closed electrolysis cell with a side-opened window. Natural seawater collected from the Yellow Sea in Dalian was used as the reaction electrolyte solution. The generated H2 was determined by a gas chromatograph (GC, China education Au-Light technology Co. Ltd., China).

MD simulations were utilized to study the distribution of Cl− ions close to the photoanode surface. Specifically, three-atom-thick (111) planes of TiO2 were established at the bottom of the box as the surface model of the TiO2 NA sample. The model for the SNCL/TiO2 NA sample was approximately constructed as a graphene-like structure with 558C, 13 N and 5 S atoms (Fig. S18). The experimental environment was simulated with a solvent model, containing 2000 water molecules, 100 Na+ ions and 100 Cl- ions. The density of the solution was 1.1 g cm−3. All the MD calculations were carried out through Materials Studio.

Typical TEM and high-resolution TEM (inset) images of the S, N-CDs are displayed in Fig. S2a. The well-dispersed S, N-CDs exhibited a spherical morphology, with sizes ranging from 2.8 to 7.6 nm and an average diameter of 5.0 nm. The lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm corresponded to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [26]. The XRD patterns of the S, N-CDs are displayed in Fig. S2b. There was only a broad peak at 26.0°, attributable to the typical crystalline phase of graphitized carbon [27]. Raman spectroscopy was carried out to further analyze the crystal structure of the S, N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S2c, the Raman spectrum of the S, N-CDs displayed a D and a G band at 1359.62 and 1565.46 cm−1, which were attributed to the disorder-induced sp3 carbon atoms and sp2 hybridized carbon, respectively. The relative intensity ratio of the peaks (ID/IG) was 0.99, which demonstrated the simultaneous existence of the sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in the S, N-CDs.

The surface functional groups and chemical composition of the S, N-CDs were analyzed via FT-IR and XPS techniques. The FT-IR spectra of the S, N-CDs are displayed in Fig. S2d. The peaks at 3171 and 3069.5 cm−1 were due to N-H/O-H and C-H stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 2057, 1736, 1363, 1057.5 and 900 cm−1 were assigned to -CNS, C=O, C-N, C-S/C-O and -SO3 bonds, respectively [28]. These results suggested that the prepared S, N-CDs contained abundant functional groups on their surface. The XPS survey spectrum is shown in Fig. S3a. Five peaks were observed at 284.8 eV for C1s, 399.9 eV for N1s, 531.6 eV for O1s, 227 eV for S2p and 163.1 eV for S2s, respectively. The elemental composition of the S, N-CDs was calculated to be 61.1 % C, 21.85 % O, 14.98 % N and 2.07 % S. These suggested that the S, N-CDs prepared in this work consisted mainly of C element and contained copious amounts of N, O and S elements. The high-resolution XPS spectra are illustrated in Figs. S3b–e. The high-resolution C1s spectrum featured three peaks at 284.6, 285.5 and 288 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O/C-S and C=O, respectively [29]. In high-resolution O1s spectrum, the peaks at 530.9, 531.6 and 532.5 eV were attributed to C=O, C-O and C-OH bonds, respectively [30]. In high-resolution N1s spectrum, the peaks located at 399.3, 399.8 and 400.3 eV corresponded to graphitic N, pyrrolic N and amino N, respectively [31]. The high-resolution S2p spectrum exhibited four peaks at 163.1, 164.1165.6, and 166.4 eV, which were attributed to alkyl S2p3/2, S2p1/2 and thiophenic S2p3/2, S2p1/2, respectively [32]. The above results further verified that the S, N-CDs contained abundant functional groups on their surface, which were well consistent with the FT-IR test results.

XRD was utilized to further study the crystal structure of the samples. Their XRD patterns are displayed in Fig. 2g. After excluding the peaks of the FTO glass (Fig. S4), the diffraction peaks at 35.42°, 41.26°, 54.58°, 61.90° and 69.70° were detected in the patterns of all the three samples, which were in well agreement with the typical XRD pattern of rutile TiO2 (PDF 88–1172). This result indicated that the modification process did not destroy the crystal structure of the TiO2 photocatalyst, which well supported the findings from the SEM and TEM images. The typical S, N-CDs signal was not obtained in SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA samples. This could be explained by the scattering effect and the tiny amount of decorated S, N-CDs, making it difficult to detect via XRD [34]. Additionally, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping was conducted to confirm the successful modification of the TiO2 NA by S, N-CDs. As shown in Figure S6 and Fig. 2h, the EDS mapping images of SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA revealed 5 elements, namely Ti, O, C, N, and S. In comparison, TiO2 NA sample only displayed two element signals, no extra element was found other than Ti and O (Fig. S5). This result indicated that the SNCL was successfully introduced into the anodic materials.

FT-IR and XPS measurements were carried out to investigate the chemical structure and the elemental composition of the samples. The FT-IR spectra of the photoanode materials are displayed in Fig. 3 a. The TiO2 NA, SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA samples all presented a O-Ti-O peak at 1750 cm−1. Extra peaks located at 1406, 1260 and 1087 cm−1 were observed in the SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA samples, indicating CN, C=O and C-S/C-O groups, respectively [35]. XPS spectra of the samples were illustrated in Fig. 3b. The characteristic peaks of Ti2p and O1s were obtained in the XPS survey spectra of all the three samples, which originated from the TiO2 photocatalyst. Two extra peaks located at 169.51/163.61 eV and 399.73/399.35 eV were observed in the spectra of the SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA samples, corresponding to S2p and N1s, respectively. The detection of the carbon-containing functional groups and the S, N elements could well prove the existence of the SNCL structure in photoanode after the modification. The high-resolution Ti2p spectra of the TiO2 NA, SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA samples could be fitted into two peaks at 464.4/464.5/464.5 eV and 458.5/458.6/458.7 eV, corresponding to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, respectively, which were attributed to the Ti4+ of TiO2 [33]. Compared with the TiO2 NA sample, the Ti2p peaks of the SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA slightly shifted into the positive binding energy direction (Fig. 3c). This could be attributed to the decrease in the electron density on the Ti atoms, caused by the high electronegativity of the C and N atoms [36]. The high-resolution O1s spectra of the TiO2 NA and SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA samples were fitted into two peaks at 531.5/531.7 eV and 529.8/530 eV, which could be assigned to Ti-O and Ti-OH (Ti-OH/C-O) bonds, respectively. In the high-resolution O1s spectrum of the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA sample, the peak located at 530 eV was attributed to the Ti-O bond, the peaks centered at 530.8 eV and 532 eV were assigned to the -OH and C-O groups, respectively [33,37]. The increased intensity of the -OH and C-O peaks could be explained as the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups of the SNCL (Fig. 3d). These results further demonstrated that the SNCL was successfully constructed in SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA samples.

The formation process of the SNCL was investigated via TG and DTG measurements. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the TG curve of the S, N-CDs suggested that there were multiple stages in their splicing process. The degree of thermal degradation in each step could be estimated through analyzing the DTG curve. The first peak in the DTG curve was observed at 120 °C, which was attributed to the loss of bound water. The second peak at 210 °C was attributed to the dehydration of the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. The third step was the decomposition of amide, sulfurous functional and hydroxyl groups, with a peak in DTG curve at 250 °C. The decomposition of the ether bonds and hydroxyl groups was the fourth step, resulting in a peak at approximately 295 °C. The broad peak at 400 °C was ascribed to the inner pyrolysis process of the SNCL [21]. Combined with the above results, the formation process of the SNCL from S, N-CDs was proposed. The schematic is illustrated in Fig. 4a. Specifically, in the initial stage, the adjacent S, N-CDs occurred a crosslink via a dehydration reaction of their surface functional groups. In the subsequent stage, with the further increase in the reaction temperature, the crosslinked S, N-CDs experienced a secondary carbonization process, eventually forming the SNCL structure [38].

PEC seawater splitting measurements were conducted in a typical three-electrode system for analyzing the catalytic activity and reaction selectivity of the samples. Considering the assessment of their stability under a higher current and the quantification of the final products, in addition to using a simulated sunlight source, a high-energy UV lamp was also utilized during the reaction. The PEC activity of the samples was measured in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. Fig. 5 a displayed the average photocurrent density of the photoanode samples with changes in the usage of the S, N-CDs precursor. The Solar-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency (STH) values of the photoanode samples were measured using the following formula:
(2)
η = J
(
1.23
‐
V
O C P
)
/ P × 100 %
where “η” was the STH value, “J” represents the photocurrent density under sunlight, “VOCP” represents the open circuit potential of the sample and “P” was the intensity of the incident light. The experimental data are summarized in Table S1. The TiO2 NA sample showed a photocurrent density of 0.74 mA cm−2 (1.23 V vs. RHE) under simulated sunlight and 5.8 mA cm−2 (1.23 V vs. RHE) under UV light, respectively. Its STH value was 0.53 %. After introducing the SNCL, the optimal SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA presented the highest photocurrent density of 1.57 and 10.6 mA cm−2 (1.23 V vs. RHE) under simulated sunlight and UV light, respectively, with a STH value of 1.1 %, which was almost two times greater than that of the pristine TiO2 NA. It could be ascribed to the S, N-CDs modification improving the transfer efficiency of the photogenerated carriers [39]. Upon further increasing the concentration of S, N-CDs from 0.25 to 5 g L−1, there was a progressive decrease in the photocurrent density of the prepared photoanodes. It might be due to the large in-layer resistance of the SNCL caused by their excessive thickness formed under a high-concentration of S, N-CDs.

Subsequently, PEC seawater splitting experiments were carried out using SSW as the electrolyte and a thionine test was carried out to detect the formation of the ClO− during the reaction. It could investigate the CER activity of the photoanode materials. The faradaic efficiency (FE) of the CER could be calculated via the following formula:
(3)
F E
(
C E R
)
% =
(
n × m × F
)
/ Q × 100 %
where “m” represents the amount of the ClO− produced during the reaction, “n” represents the number of the electrons transferred, “F” represents the Faraday constant and “Q” represents the total charge through the circuit [7]. The experimental data are summarized in Table S2. As shown in Fig. 5b, the pristine TiO2 NA sample showed a FE (CER) of 88.3 %, indicating that it had a higher reaction selectivity towards CER. After being composited with the SNCL the FE (CER) of the samples was significantly reduced. The lowest ClO− formation activity was obtained from the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA sample, with an FE (CER) of 35.6 %. Meanwhile it maintained an acceptable photocurrent density of 0.54 mA cm−2 (1.23 V vs. RHE) under sunlight and 4.2 mA cm−2 (1.23 V vs. RHE) under UV light. These results supported that the modification of the SNCL could induce photoanode to attain an inhibitory effect on CER in seawater conditions.

Pure TiO2 NA, SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA with the optimal PEC activity and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA with the best CER inhibition performance were selected for further testing. As shown in Fig. 5c, the photovoltage of the TiO2 NA, SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA was 0.154, 0.263 and 0.124 V, respectively. Evidently, SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA exhibited a significantly higher photovoltage. This suggested that it possessed a greater driving force for promoting charge separation, which was consistent with the results of PEC performance tests. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and the transient photocurrent response curves of the samples were displayed in Fig. 5d and e. SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA showed a higher photocurrent density and all the samples exhibited excellent photo-response capabilities. Additionally, EIS measurements were carried out to further understand the interfacial charge transfer kinetics of the photoanode samples. A proper equivalent circuit contributes to analyzing and extracting information from EIS data. Therefore, an equivalent circuit model was fitted to clearly investigate their charge transfer behaviors, as illustrated in Fig. 5f (inset) [40]. Three electrical elements including a series resistance (R1), a constant phase element (CPE) and an interfacial charge transfer resistance (R2) were introduced into this equivalent circuit model. As shown in Fig. 5f, the EIS Nyquist plot of the SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA exhibited a smallest radius, implying that it had a lowest charge-transfer resistance among the samples [41]. This result demonstrated that the SNCL constructed by splicing S, N-CDs in a moderate dosage could enhance the charge separation efficiency of the photoanode materials. However, the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA showed a higher charge-transfer resistance, suggesting that the SNCL formed by excess S, N-CDs led to more sluggish charge-transfer kinetics. These results were also consistent with the results of the PEC activity test.

Subsequently, a continuous operation test was carried out to examine the long-term stability of the samples. The results were shown in Fig. 6 a. It could be seen that during the experiment, there was almost no change in the photocurrent density of the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA sample. In contrast, TiO2 NA and SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA samples suffered an obvious decline in their current densities, only remaining at 77.8 % and 81.9 % of their initial state, respectively. Moreover, the EIS plots of the samples before and after the reaction were presented in Fig. S9. It was found that the EIS characteristics of each sample still showed only one capacitive loop, the radii of TiO2 NA and SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA increased, while in contrast, the impedance arc radius of SNCL 5/TiO2 NA remained stable. This suggested that the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA demonstrated better corrosion resistance than the other samples [42]. The improvement from constructing a SNCL in the photocurrent stability could be explained by their protective effect on photoanode materials. It could prevent the corrosion of photocatalyst caused by the Cl− intrusion. Thus, the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA sample obtained the best long-term operation stability. In addition, multiple cycle experiments were conducted to investigate the CER-inhibition performance stability of the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA. It was found that the heat treatment temperature in the SNCL formation process might be a crucial factor for the stability of their CER inhibition performance. If there was an absence of the pyrolysis splicing process, the S, N-CDs would rapidly shed from the photoanode materials during the PEC measurement, failing to maintain stabilize. As shown in Fig. 6b, the sample prepared at a pyrolysis temperature of 400 °C showed a clearly increasing FE (CER) as the cyclic testing proceeded, multiplying from 29.3 % to 86.3 %. This value was comparable to that of the pristine TiO2 NA sample. In contrast, the sample prepared at 500 °C maintained a consistent FE (CER) over more repeated cycles of the experiment, demonstrating a superior stability in CER inhibition performance (Fig. 6c). However, when the pyrolyzing temperature was further increased to 600 °C, the conductive layer of the FTO glass substrate would be severely damaged, leading to an inactivation of the photoanode. Therefore, the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA sample pyrolyzed at 500 °C was considered to achieve the best long-term operation stability and CER inhibition stability, which was expected to be selected for practical application.

Envisioned in the future, industrial seawater photoelectrodes require to work under sunlight. Therefore, in order to evaluate the value in practical applications of the photoanode materials, a PEC hydrogen production system was established using the as-prepared SNCL 5/TiO2 NA photoanode. Its H2 production rate was tested in natural seawater and simulated sunlight irradiation conditions. As depicted in Fig. S10, H2 was promptly released upon the activation of the light source for SNCL 5/TiO2 NA, featuring an evolution rate of 13.0 μmol cm−2 h−1. It was at the same levels as that previously reported (Table S3). Moreover, it could remain a nearly constant activity throughout three cycle tests, with a hydrogen production rate of 11.7 and 12.2 μmol cm−2 h−1. This result signified that the as-prepared SNCL 5/TiO2 NA photoanode demonstrated a satisfactory H2 production activity and stability under practical application conditions.

Firstly, the origin of the opposite effects of SNCL formed by different concentrations of S, N-CDs (positive for SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and negative for SNCL 5/TiO2 NA) on PEC activity was investigated. As shown in Figs. S11 and S12, the DRS results demonstrated that the band gap energies of the TiO2 NA, SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA and SNCL 5/TiO2 NA were 3.1, 2.9 and 2.85 eV, respectively. According to the Mott-Schottky plots, the conduction band (CB) position of the samples could be estimated by determining the flat band potentials as −0.65, −0.6 and −0.53 eV, respectively (Fig. S13) [43,44]. It could be seen that the presence of the SNCL structure could slightly narrow the band gap of the photoanode materials by reducing their CB potential. Furthermore, their VB positions could be calculated as 2.35, 2.3 and 2.32 eV, respectively. The SNCL 5/TiO2 NA and SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA samples only showed a negligible change in the VB position compared to the pure TiO2 NA sample, which was not sufficient to affect their PEC activity and CER performance (Fig. S14). This was corroborated by the results of the photocurrent density test of the samples under visible light irradiation (Fig. S15). Additionally, the carrier density (Nd) of the samples could be estimated according to the equation (eq. S(1)) [45,46]. SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA showed the smallest Mott-Schottky slope, indicating the largest carrier density. It could be due to the hole-accepting properties of the S, N-CDs in SNCL, which was consistent with past reports [47–49]. This result suggested that the enhancement on PEC activity could be attributed to the improvement of the SNCL formed by a low dosage of the S, N-CDs on carrier transport capacity. However, the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA sample exhibited the lowest carrier density. This could be explained by the stacking of the excess S, N-CDs, which resulted in an increased internal resistance of the SNCL. Moreover, PL spectroscopy measurements were carried out to confirm the improvement on carrier transport capacity of the SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA sample. As shown in Fig. S16, the PL intensity of the SNCL 0.25/TiO2 NA sample was obviously lower than that of the pristine TiO2 NA sample. This result further supported the ability of the SNCL, which was constructed in a low concentration of S, N-CDs, to inhibit the recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs [48].

Following that, the mechanism regarding the CER inhibition ability of the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA was investigated in subsequent experiments. Analyzing the electric double layer (EDL) structure of the photoanode materials is conducive to understanding the contact behavior of the Cl− ions with their surfaces. It can provide clues for proposing the CER inhibition mechanism of SNCL. Since he zero charge points of rutile TiO2 is at pH 4, the remaining negative charges would accumulate on its surface under neutral SSW conditions (pH 6.9) [50]. Normally, hydrated Na+ would be gravitated to form a Stern layer and the Cl− ions would be distributed in the diffusion layer. Zeta potential measurements were utilized to examine the influence of the SNCL on the behaviors of the charged ions in EDL of the photoanode materials. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after constructing SNCL via a high concentration of the S, N-CDs, the zeta potential increased from −29.58 mV to −17.36 mV. According to the principle of zeta potential measurement, the charged ions would form a slip plane, within which the ions do not move relative to the surface of the materials during the testing process. It usually locates in the diffuse layer very close to the Stern layer of the materials. The measured potential value of this slip plane with respect to the ionic equilibrium position is considered to be the zeta potential (Fig. 7b). Therefore, the increase in zeta potential could be elucidated by the presence of SNCL moving Cl− away from the electrode, resulting in more positively charged Na+ being squeezed into the inner side of the slip plane. This result could well demonstrate the repulsive effect of the SNCL on Cl− ions, accounting for the more difficult reaction kinetics of the CER on SNCL 5/TiO2 NA photoanode. In addition, diffusion of the Cl− ions near the photoanode materials was simulated via MD calculations. As shown in Fig. 7c, most of the Cl− ions aggregated 1–3 nm from the surface of the pure TiO2 NA sample but tended to be located more than 4 nm away from the SNCL 5/TiO2 NA sample. Their ion distribution models and reaction mechanism schematic of the are illustrated in Figure S17 and Fig. 7d. For TiO2 NA sample, Cl− would approach the electrode material and adsorb on its surface, causing a higher CER activity. The introducing of the SNCL could repel Cl− ions, thereby significantly reducing their probability of being adsorbed on the surface of the materials. It effectively inhibited the oxidation of Cl− and the corrosion of equipment. These calculation results agree well with the experimental findings.

Making a deeper discussion for the function of the S, N element in the SNCL aids in further understand the internal mechanism of its CER inhibiting performance. A series of the Sx, N-CDs with different S contents and their corresponding SxNCL/TiO2 NA photoanodes were prepared in further experiments. As displayed in Table S4, the surface S contents of the N-CDs; S0.5, N-CDs; S1, N-CDs; S2, N-CDs; and S3, N-CDs samples were 0 %, 1.5 %, 2.07 %, 2.09 % and 3.14 %, respectively. There were no noticeable changes in the elemental types and surface groups of the various samples detecting from the XPS and FT-IR spectra (Figs. S19–S24). Moreover, according to the TEM images of the various Sx, N-CDs samples, an increase in the S source precursor usage would lead to a larger average particle size of them (Figs. S26–S29). Besides, it was obtained in their XRD patterns that the samples with a higher S contents had a higher graphitization degree, which might be due to the larger size of their graphitized carbon core (Fig. S25). XPS spectra of their corresponding photoanodes are displayed in Figs. S30–S34. No S2p signal was detected in the XPS spectrum of the N-CDs/TiO2 NA. Besides that, there are no significant differences in the XPS spectra of the SxNCL/TiO2 NA samples. This indicated the similar elemental composition and chemical structure of them. More detailed discussions of their microscopic morphology and chemical composition were described in the Supporting Information.

The PEC activity and CER inhibition performance tests of the various photoanodes were carried out. Their results are displayed in Fig. S35. It was found that as the S content increased, the FE (CER) of the photoanode decreased from 87.4 % to 19.5 %. These results indicated that the N element in SNCL seemed to have no impact on its CER-inhibiting performance, while the S element might be the crucial factor in inhibiting CER activity. However, at excessively high S contents, the SNCL also decreased the PEC activity of the photoanode, such that the S3NCL/TiO2 NA sample only showed a photocurrent density of 1.49 mA cm−2 (1.23 eV vs. RHE) under UV light. This might be due to the fact that excessive doping of S element would lead to a further decrease in the electrical conductivity (Fig. S36). Therefore, an appropriate S ratio was important to achieve the balance between the CER-inhibiting performance and PEC activity for the photoanode materials.

In summary, a CER inhibiting strategy was proposed in this study, involving splicing of S, N-CDs to construct a SNCL on TiO2 NA photoanode. The SNCL could function as a barrier to prevent Cl− ions from the TiO2 photocatalyst, adjusting the EDL structure of the photoanode. It effectively inhibited the harmful CER occurrence, inducing photoanode to achieve a higher reaction selectivity and a more reliable durability. The as-prepared SNCL 5/TiO2 NA sample exhibited a FE (CER) of 35.6 %, less than two-fifths of the premodification level (88.3 %), meanwhile remained an acceptable-level photocurrent density of 4.2 mA cm−2 (1.23 V vs. RHE) under UV light. The S element in the SNCL was proved to be the crucial factor for its CER-inhibiting ability. Through further regulation of the S content within the S, N-CDs precursor to a higher level, a lower FE (CER) of 19.5 % could be achieved on the photoanode. The findings in this work provide a potential approach to solve the problem of the harmful CER in seawater splitting process and also propose insights for the design of photoanodes for seawater splitting.
